title: Game Server Patterns for Multi-Agent Coordination

slides.heading: Game Server Patterns for Multi-Agent Coordination
slides.subtitle: What 20 years of MMO architecture teaches us about multi-agent AI — Iteration 1 Synthesis

slides.heading: The Isomorphism
slides.body:
MMO game servers and multi-agent AI frameworks solve the **same structural problem**: multiple concurrent processes mutating shared state.

- **Game world** = Filesystem
- **Per-client replica** = Agent's context window
- **Game server** = Agent instance
- **Replication layer** = MCP coordination server
- **EntityGraph** = Git repository
- **Tick rate** = Agent sync rhythm
- **Authority** = Write lock / task assignment
- **Interest management** = Context engineering

slides.heading: Finding #1: The Single-Writer Invariant
slides.body:
Every system examined enforces one rule: **exactly one node has write authority per entity/component at any moment.**

- Star Citizen, SpatialOS, EVE Online, Photon, Mirror, coherence — all enforce this independently
- SpatialOS innovated **component-level authority**: different workers own different components of the same entity
- **For agents**: different agents can write to different sections of the same file, but never the same section simultaneously

slides.heading: Finding #2: Git IS Already the Netcode

left.body:
**Game Pattern**
- Client-side prediction
- Server reconciliation
- State snapshots
- Delta compression
- Rollback + replay
- Conflict detection
- Optimistic concurrency

right.body:
**Git Equivalent**
- Working in a branch/worktree
- `git rebase`
- Commits
- Git's diff algorithm
- `git rebase --onto`
- Merge conflicts
- Push fails if diverged

slides.heading: Finding #3: The Missing Layer
slides.body:
Star Citizen's **replication layer** (dedicated microservice tier between servers) is the pattern Sherpa needs.

- No server talks to another server directly — all state flows through the intermediary
- Handles authority tracking, transfer mediation, crash recovery
- Current AI frameworks (OpenAI Swarm, Semantic Kernel) transfer conversational context, not artifact authority
- **Sherpa's MCP server IS the replication layer** — this is the gap no one else fills

slides.heading: Finding #4: Context Engineering = Interest Management
slides.body:
Game industry solved "what state does this client need" 30 years ago. AI calls it "context engineering."

**The DOI Model for Files** (from Eclipse Mylyn, 2005):
```
DOI(file, agent) = TaskRelevance + DependencyProximity - Staleness
```

**Three LOD Tiers:**
- **LOD 0**: Full file content (editing + direct deps)
- **LOD 1**: Signatures/types (~10-20% of tokens)
- **LOD 2**: File paths only (~1% of tokens)

slides.heading: Finding #5: Prevention > Detection > Compensation
slides.body:
The cost hierarchy for multi-agent conflicts:

| Strategy | Cost | Example |
|----------|------|--------|
| **Prevention** | Cheap | Task partitioning, file leases |
| **Detection** | Medium | Git version check at merge |
| **Compensation** | Expensive | Rebase, retry, Judge arbitration |

Fighting games invest in prediction accuracy to minimize rollback. Sherpa should invest in task partitioning to minimize conflicts. **Re-running an agent session costs minutes.**

left.body:
**1. Single-Writer Authority**
Component-level, MCP-enforced

**2. Replication Layer**
MCP server mediates all mutations

**3. Prevention→Compensation**
Partition first, detect second, compensate last

right.body:
**4. Bounded Authority Transfer**
Fencing tokens + timeout + orphan detection

**5. Time Dilation as Backpressure**
Slow down under load, don't add more agents

slides.heading: Next: Two Branch Seeds
slides.body:
**DOI Model for Agents** (high priority)
- Prototype a DOI-based context selector as MCP tool
- Input: task description + focus files → Output: ranked file list with LOD assignments
- Measure against real agent sessions

**Game Authority as MCP Protocol** (high priority)
- Translate Improbable patent's 4-state model into MCP tool API
- Design fencing token implementation backed by SQLite
- Define heartbeat/orphan detection protocol
